docs.title: project_name
docs.text:
{{project_description}}

A production-ready Apache 2.0 template that gives you a fully structured, documented, and CI/CD-enabled starting point — so you can focus on building, not scaffolding.

Get Started [View on GitHub](https://github.com/{{organization}}/project_name){ .md-button }

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
This section contains runnable code examples that demonstrate real-world usage of project_name. Each example is self-contained and can be copied directly into your own project.

[!NOTE] All examples assume you have completed Installation. Replace YOUR_KEY and other placeholder values with your own credentials before running.

docs.title: Example Categories
docs.text:
• :material-rocket-launch-outline: Basic Examples

Simple, minimal examples that demonstrate the core API with no extra setup required. The best place to start.

Coming soon.

  • :material-tune-variant: Advanced Examples

Examples that demonstrate more complex patterns, custom configurations, and production-grade usage.

Coming soon.

  • :material-console: CLI Examples

Examples using the project_name command-line interface, including scripting and automation patterns.

Coming soon.

  • :material-docker: Docker Examples

Containerised usage examples, including Docker Compose configurations for common deployment scenarios.

Coming soon.

Contributing an Example

Have a useful snippet or pattern to share? See the Contributing Guide to learn how to add a new example to this section.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Welcome to project_name! This section walks you through everything you need to go from zero to productive.

Follow the pages in order for the best experience, or jump directly to the section you need.

docs.title: In This Section
docs.text:
• :material-download-outline: Installation

Install project_name via pip, from source, or using Docker. Includes platform-specific notes and verification steps.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Installation Guide

  • :material-play-circle-outline: Quick Start

Your first 5 minutes with project_name. Initialize, run a command, and see results — fast.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Quick Start

  • :material-transit-connection-variant: Workflows

End-to-end walkthroughs for the most common user stories and usage patterns.

:octicons-arrow-right-24: Workflows

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page covers all supported installation methods for project_name.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Before installing, ensure your system meets the following prerequisites:

Requirement Minimum Version Notes Python 3.10+ Required for all installation methods Package Manager pip or Hatch Required for dependencies Git 2.x Required for source installs Docker 24.x Optional — for containerized installs

docs.title: Standard Installation
docs.text:
The recommended way to install project_name for most users:

pip install project_name

docs.title: Verify the Installation
docs.text:
After installation, confirm it is working correctly:

project_name --version

You should see output similar to:

project_name 0.1.0

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
To install the latest unreleased code directly from the repository:

git clone https://github.com/{{organization}}/project_name.git
cd project_name

# Install dependencies for development
# If you are using hatch (recommended):
pipx install hatch
hatch shell

# Or using pip directly:
pip install -e .

[!TIP] This is the recommended setup for contributors looking to make changes to the source code.

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text:
A pre-built Docker image is available for containerized environments:

# Pull the latest image
docker pull ghcr.io/{{organization}}/project_name:latest

# Run a one-off command
docker run --rm ghcr.io/{{organization}}/project_name:latest project_name --version

For a persistent, volume-mounted setup using Docker Compose, see the docker-compose.yml in the root of the repository.

docs.title: Platform-Specific Notes
docs.text:
macOS 
Python and `pip` or `hatch` work seamlessly on macOS. We recommend using `brew install python` if you need a base Python environment.
Linux 
Use your distribution's package manager to install Python 3.10+ (e.g., `sudo apt install python3`).
Windows 
Ensure Python is added to your PATH during the Windows installer setup.

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To upgrade an existing installation to the latest release:

pip install --upgrade project_name

docs.title: Uninstalling
docs.text: pip uninstall project_name

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Problem Solution command not found: project_name Ensure the binaries directory is on your $PATH. Import errors after install Ensure you have the latest version installed. Version conflicts Isolate your dependencies using your language's recommended tool.

If you continue to experience issues, please visit our Support page.

Next: Quick Start →

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
This guide gets you from a fresh installation to running your first command in under 5 minutes.

[!NOTE] Make sure you have completed Installation before continuing.

docs.title: Step 1 — Initialize Your Environment
docs.text:
If you haven't already, set up your project and install project_name:

python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install project_name

docs.title: Step 2 — Verify the Install
docs.text:
project_name --version

Expected output:

project_name 0.1.0

docs.title: Step 3 — Run Your First Command
docs.text:
from project_name import Client

# Initialize the client
client = Client(api_key="YOUR_KEY")

# Run a core action
result = client.run_action("hello_world")
print(result)

Expected output:

[INFO] Initializing project_name client...
[SUCCESS] Action completed! Result: Hello, World from project_name!

[!TIP] Replace "YOUR_KEY" with your actual API key or credentials. See the Reference for all available client configuration options.

docs.title: Step 4 — Explore Further
docs.text:
Now that your first command works, explore what project_name can do:

  • Workflows — Common end-to-end usage patterns
  • Guides — Task-specific deep dives
  • Reference — Full API and CLI documentation
  • Examples — Runnable code examples

Next: Workflows →

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text:
This page walks through the most common end-to-end usage patterns for project_name. Each workflow is a self-contained user story — pick the one that matches your use case.

[!NOTE] These are placeholder workflows. Replace each section with the real workflows that users of your project will follow.

docs.title: Workflow 1 — Basic Usage
docs.text: Goal: Perform the most common, standard operation with project_name.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Initialize the client
from project_name import Client
client = Client(api_key="YOUR_KEY")
  1. Configure your inputs
config = {"mode": "fast", "retries": 3}
  1. Execute the action
result = client.run_action("hello_world", config=config)
  1. Handle the output
print(f"Success: {result}")

docs.title: Workflow 2 — Docker-Based Execution
docs.text: Goal: Run project_name in a containerized environment without any local setup.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Pull the image
docker pull ghcr.io/{{organization}}/project_name:latest
  1. Run with environment variables
docker run --rm \
  -e API_KEY="YOUR_KEY" \
  -v $(pwd)/output:/app/output \
  ghcr.io/{{organization}}/project_name:latest \
  project_name run --output /app/output
  1. Inspect the output
ls ./output/

docs.title: Workflow 3 — CI/CD Integration
docs.text:
Goal: Integrate project_name into a GitHub Actions pipeline.

# .github/workflows/run.yml
name: Run project_name

on: [push]

jobs:
  run:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up environment
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.10"

      - name: Install project_name
        run: |
          pip install .

      - name: Run action
        env:
          API_KEY: ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}
        run: project_name run --config config.yaml

Need more? See the Guides section for in-depth task-specific documentation or browse the Examples for runnable code.

docs.title: Generating API Reference Docs
docs.text: This template is pre-configured to work with mkdocstrings for auto-generating API documentation from docstrings.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
[!NOTE] mkdocstrings supports multiple languages via handlers (e.g., Python, C++, Crystal). See the mkdocstrings documentation to configure it for your language.

  1. The plugin and its Python handler are already managed by Hatch in the docs environment (mkdocstrings[python]).
  2. Configure mkdocs.yml according to your language handler and create your reference pages.

docs.title: Generating CLI Reference Docs
docs.text:
For CLI documentation, consider using one of the following approaches depending on your CLI framework:

Framework Plugin Notes Typer typer-cli Generates markdown from Typer apps Click mkdocs-click Auto-generates docs from Click decorators Argparse Manual Document commands in a dedicated cli.md page

Template Reminder

Replace this page with actual generated reference documentation as your project matures. The placeholders above are intentional starting points.
